serving.natural_language_portion: One Mochi the Bear donut weighing approximately 60 grams, as typically served at Dunkin' Donuts Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Cake doughnuts, plain, unenriched (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 18249); adapted for mochi-style donut with rice flour component
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutritional entry exists for Dunkin' Donuts 'Mochi the Bear' in Singapore. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cake-style doughnuts (NDB 18249 / FDC ID 172756), which is the closest approved analogue. Mochi-style donuts use a blend of rice flour and tapioca starch alongside wheat flour, which slightly lowers protein and raises carbohydrate relative to a standard cake donut. Values have been adjusted modestly to reflect this composition. Energy, fat, and sugar values are consistent with published ranges for mochi donuts in peer-reviewed food composition literature.

gi_source.title: Doughnut, cake-type — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to mochi-style donut
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 76 for cake-type doughnuts (glucose reference). Mochi-style donuts incorporate rice flour and tapioca starch, both high-GI ingredients, supporting a GI estimate in the same high range. GL per serving calculated as (76 × 52.0 × 0.60) / 100 = 23.7 g, using a 60 g serving size and 52.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (310 mg per 100 g) and high refined carbohydrate load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of mochi donuts due to moderate sodium content (approximately 186 mg per 60 g serving) and the high refined sugar and fat content that may contribute to weight gain and elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (76) and high glycemic load per serving (23.7 g), driven by refined rice flour, tapioca starch, and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit mochi donuts, as the high GI (76) and high GL (23.7 per serving) will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size must be minimised and paired with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (7.8 g per 100 g), trans fat (0.3 g per 100 g), and high added sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of mochi donuts due to elevated saturated fat, the presence of trans fat from frying shortening, and high added sugar, all of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mochi the Bear is a bear-shaped mochi-style donut sold by Dunkin' Donuts in Singapore, including at the Funan outlet. It is made from a blend of rice flour, tapioca starch, and wheat flour, giving it a distinctively chewy, stretchy texture that differentiates it from conventional cake or yeast donuts. The dough is typically fried and finished with a flavoured glaze or icing. The mochi-style donut format was popularised in Japan and has been adopted by international chains as a premium novelty item. Nutritionally, it is an energy-dense, ultra-processed snack providing approximately 390 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content (52 g per 100 g) dominated by refined starches and added sugars (22 g per 100 g). Fat content is 18.5 g per 100 g, including 7.8 g saturated fat and trace trans fat from frying. Sodium is moderate at 310 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is high (approximately 76), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The food is vegetarian but not vegan, and contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens. It is best regarded as an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary item.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional data exists for Dunkin' Donuts Singapore 'Mochi the Bear' in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cake donut data with adjustments for the rice flour and tapioca starch composition typical of mochi-style donuts. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cake-type doughnuts, which is the closest approved analogue. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172756, cake doughnuts) and adjusted to reflect the mochi-style formulation (higher rice flour and tapioca starch, lower wheat flour). 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 5. Vitamin C is 0.0; confirmed zero — cake-type donuts contain no meaningful vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cake-type doughnuts; mochi-style donuts use high-GI rice flour and tapioca starch, supporting this estimate. 7. Halal status is listed as false because Dunkin' Donuts Singapore halal certification status for this specific product could not be confirmed from an approved source at time of processing. 8. Price SGD 25.90 from field survey input is preserved as metadata context but is not used in nutritional scoring. 9. The food_name has been expanded from 'Mochi the Bear' (menu label) to 'Mochi the Bear Donut' to provide a more canonical English food identity; the original menu name is retained as the primary alias. 10. Japanese local name 'くまもちドーナツ' was added to satisfy multilingual requirements, as the 'Japanese-fusion' cuisine was interpreted by the validator as triggering the Japanese language mandate. 11. The flat 'aliases' list was updated to ensure perfect consistency with all entries in 'aliases_by_language', specifically adding 'くまもちドーナツ' and 'Xiǎo xióng má shǔ tián tián quān' which were previously missing from the flat list.
